comments:
This paper describes the Lingban system for the 3rd CHiME challenge. It proposes a multi-channel time–frequency masking front-end that combines magnitude-squared coherence (MSC) and phase-difference measurement (PDM) masks applied after an MVDR beamformer, together with a two-stage microphone self-calibration to mitigate phase mismatch. The back-end integrates strong, well-established techniques: fMLLR and online i-vectors, maxout and LSTMP acoustic models with sMBR sequence training, a 4-gram+silence-probability lexicon, RNNME rescoring, and lattice combination. Experiments on the official CHiME data show substantial relative WER reductions (reported >57% on real data compared to the best baseline) and the paper provides ablation-style comparisons isolating front-end, acoustic modeling, and language modeling contributions.

Strengths:
- Practical system that combines multiple complementary, state-of-the-art components and demonstrates large WER gains on the CHiME dataset.
- The proposed MSC+PDM masking and microphone self-calibration are sensible and empirically validated; results include comparisons that show the contribution of each component.
- Use of modern acoustic and language modeling techniques (LSTMP, i-vectors, sMBR, RNNME) is appropriate and well integrated.

Weaknesses / Issues to address:
- Novelty is incremental: most individual components are known; contribution is primarily in their integration and empirical tuning for CHiME. The paper should make clearer which parts are novel versus adapted from prior work.
- Some technical details are missing or sparse (exact mask fusion strategy, masking thresholds, detailed calibration filter specifications, hyperparameters for networks, training schedules, and computational cost/runtime). These details are important for reproducibility.
- The manuscript contains some repetition and minor editorial issues; clarity would benefit from tighter organization and more concise writing.
- The analysis of when/why PDM or MSC help (e.g., in which noise or reverberation conditions) is limited. More diagnostic analysis (spectrogram examples, per-condition breakdowns beyond the overall WERs) would strengthen the paper.
- Phase calibration is promising but only briefly explored (authors note limited time). More thorough evaluation of calibration impact and robustness would be valuable.

Overall assessment and recommendation:
The paper presents a strong engineering effort and a well-performing system for a realistic multi-microphone ASR challenge. While the technical novelty is modest, the empirical gains are significant and the ablation comparisons are useful. Prior to publication, I recommend the authors add more implementation details to improve reproducibility, tighten the text to remove repetition, and expand analysis of when the proposed front-end helps (conditions, failure modes, and computational considerations). Given the clear empirical contributions to noise-robust ASR, I recommend acceptance.
